author: Alkarim Billawala / alkarim.billawala.ca
weeks: Week 33 · KU I — Renal Function, AKI & CKD

questions.stem: A 68-year-old with creatinine 550 (baseline 130), K 7.4, venous pH 7.05, pulmonary edema, and anuria remains hyperkalemic (7.2) and overloaded despite IV insulin/glucose and high-dose loop diuretics. Most definitive next step?
questions.explain: Refractory hyperkalemia + severe acidosis (pH <7.1) + volume overload not responding to medical therapy are absolute dialysis indications (AEIOU). Resins/diuretics have failed → urgent hemodialysis.

questions.stem: A 76-year-old on IV levofloxacin for pneumonia has creatinine rise from 90 to 280 on day 7; she is euvolemic. Urinalysis: 1+ protein, blood, WBCs and WBC casts; FENa 2.5%. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: AKI ~7 days after a fluoroquinolone with WBCs and WBC casts is classic acute interstitial nephritis (allergic, drug-induced). ATN would show muddy-brown granular casts. Treatment is to stop the drug.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old with 15-year T2DM and hypertension on metformin, amlodipine, atorvastatin has eGFR 42 and urine ACR 150 mg/mmol. Which addition best delays CKD progression?
questions.explain: Proteinuric diabetic CKD → an SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin) slows progression (DAPA-CKD). An ACEi/ARB would also be strongly indicated here (he's on neither). Diltiazem/metoprolol/chlorthalidone lower BP but aren't specifically renoprotective.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old with fatigue, joint pain, new hemoptysis, BP 170/95, heavy hematuria with RBC casts has a NEGATIVE ANA and NORMAL C3/C4. Which mechanism is most likely?
options: Pauci-immune injury with autoantibodies to neutrophil cytoplasm (ANCA)
questions.explain: A pulmonary-renal nephritic syndrome with negative ANA and normal complement is pauci-immune ANCA-associated vasculitis (PR3/MPO antibodies). Low complement would suggest SLE; PLA2R is nephrotic (membranous).

questions.stem: A 30-year-old at 24 weeks gestation has BP 155/95 on repeat readings (gestational hypertension). Which antihypertensive is strictly contraindicated?
questions.explain: ACE inhibitors (ramipril) and ARBs are teratogenic (renal dysgenesis) and contraindicated in pregnancy. Safe options are labetalol, methyldopa, and nifedipine XL.

questions.stem: A patient with ESRD missing dialysis has fatigue, pruritus, and bone pain. Which lab profile fits the expected mineral bone disorder?
options: High Ca, low phosphate, low PTH
Low Ca, low phosphate, high PTH
Low Ca, high phosphate, high PTH
High Ca, high phosphate, low PTH
questions.explain: CKD-MBD: loss of 1-α-hydroxylase → less active vitamin D → hypocalcemia; failure to excrete phosphate → hyperphosphatemia; both stimulate PTH → secondary hyperparathyroidism (high PTH).

guide.s: low Ca, high PO4, high PTH

questions.stem: A 10-year-old has 1 week of periorbital and leg edema, normal BP, urine dipstick 4+ protein with NO blood. Best next step?
options: Start oral prednisone 1 mg/kg with close follow-up
ACE inhibitor + loop diuretic, reassess in 4 weeks
questions.explain: Childhood nephrotic syndrome (heavy proteinuria, edema, no hematuria, normal BP) is almost always minimal change disease, which is steroid-responsive → start empiric prednisone; biopsy only if it fails to respond.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old has 4 months of fatigue/weight loss, BP 185/110, creatinine 510 (baseline 85), new anemia, heavy hematuria with RBC casts and 3 g/L proteinuria. Which statement is most accurate?
options: Empiric IV corticosteroids should be started urgently to preserve renal function
Withhold treatment until biopsy confirms the etiology
Start hemodialysis immediately to prevent nephron loss
A podocyte foot-process defect is the mechanism
questions.explain: Nephritic syndrome with RBC casts and a rapidly rising creatinine = rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, a renal emergency. Give urgent empiric IV (pulse) corticosteroids — do not delay for biopsy.

questions.stem: A 71-year-old is anuric (0 mL urine over 12 h) on post-op day 3 with creatinine 310 (baseline 95). What is his approximate eGFR?
questions.explain: An anuric kidney filters nothing, so the true eGFR is 0. Estimating equations are invalid because they assume a steady-state creatinine, which isn't true in acute evolving AKI.

questions.stem: A man with stage 4 CKD has Hb 88 (baseline 105), adequate iron, and exertional dyspnea. Starting an ESA, what Hb target is appropriate?
questions.explain: ESA is indicated for symptomatic anemia with Hb <100; the target is 110–120 g/L. Normalizing Hb (>130) increases stroke risk, so it is avoided.

questions.stem: A dehydrated patient has AKI with bland urine, urine sodium 12 mmol/L, and FENa 0.5%. Which AKI category is this and what does the FENa indicate?
options: Pre-renal; intact tubules avidly reabsorbing sodium
questions.explain: A FENa <1% with bland urine indicates intact tubules reabsorbing sodium hard in response to hypoperfusion = pre-renal AKI. Restore perfusion (volume) and it reverses.

questions.stem: A patient develops AKI after prolonged hypotension and IV contrast. Urine microscopy shows muddy-brown granular casts; FENa is 3%. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: Ischemia/nephrotoxin (contrast) injury with muddy-brown granular casts and FENa >1% = acute tubular necrosis. The high FENa reflects tubules that can no longer reabsorb sodium.

questions.stem: An adult with nephrotic-range proteinuria has antibodies against the phospholipase A2 receptor on podocytes and subepithelial immune deposits on biopsy. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: Adult nephrotic syndrome with anti-PLA2R antibodies and subepithelial immune complexes ('spike and dome') is membranous nephropathy, the classic primary nephrotic disease of adults.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old develops visible hematuria 1–2 days after an upper respiratory infection, with mildly elevated BP and normal creatinine. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: Hematuria concurrent with (within 1–2 days of) an upper respiratory infection — synpharyngitic — in a young adult is classic IgA nephropathy, the most common glomerulonephritis worldwide.

questions.stem: Which finding meets the definition of chronic kidney disease?
options: eGFR <60 or kidney damage markers present for ≥3 months
Serum creatinine above the lab range once
questions.explain: CKD requires an eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73m² OR markers of kidney damage (e.g. albuminuria) for at least 3 months — chronicity is essential to distinguish it from AKI.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old with new hypertension and T2DM wants the most effective single lifestyle change. What is the BP target and best lifestyle advice?
options: <130/80; the DASH diet gives the greatest BP reduction
questions.explain: With diabetes the BP target is <130/80. Among lifestyle changes, the DASH diet provides the greatest BP-lowering benefit (though sodium restriction, weight loss, and exercise all help).

questions.stem: An older man with AKI, a distended bladder, and a history of urinary hesitancy is suspected of obstruction. What is the first-line investigation?
questions.explain: Suspected post-renal (obstructive) AKI is investigated first with a renal/bladder ultrasound to detect hydronephrosis; relieving the obstruction (e.g. catheter for BPH) can reverse the AKI.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old with hypertension, progressive GFR decline, minimal proteinuria, and a strong family history of dialysis is found to have bilateral enlarged cystic kidneys. Which extrarenal complication should be considered?
questions.explain: ADPKD (family history, big cysts, minimal proteinuria) is associated with hepatic cysts and intracranial (berry) aneurysms — screen selected patients given the risk of subarachnoid hemorrhage.

questions.stem: Before prescribing a renally-cleared antibiotic to an 84-year-old woman with a 'normal' serum creatinine, what is the most important step?
options: Prescribe the standard dose; creatinine is normal
Estimate GFR (creatinine can be deceptively normal with low muscle mass)
questions.explain: Low muscle mass keeps creatinine 'normal' despite a 30–50% drop in functional glomeruli with age. Always estimate GFR before dosing renally-cleared drugs in older adults to avoid toxicity.

questions.stem: Two weeks after starting ramipril for proteinuric CKD, a patient's creatinine has risen 22% and potassium is 5.0. What is the appropriate action?
options: Stop the ACE inhibitor — this is nephrotoxicity
Continue and monitor; a <30% creatinine rise is acceptable
questions.explain: A creatinine rise of <30% after starting an ACEi/ARB is expected and acceptable (reflects reduced intraglomerular pressure), provided potassium is tolerable. Continue and monitor; the drug is renoprotective.

cards.prompt: Why is serum creatinine used to estimate GFR, and what are its caveats?
cards.answer: It is endogenous and freely filtered; but it is also secreted by the proximal tubule and varies with age/sex/muscle mass, and estimating equations require a steady state.

cards.prompt: What is the eGFR of an anuric patient in acute AKI?
cards.answer: Effectively 0 mL/min — the kidney filters nothing; estimating equations are invalid because creatinine isn't at steady state.

cards.prompt: Which kidney hormones explain CKD's hypertension, anemia, and bone disease?
cards.answer: Renin (RAAS → hypertension), erythropoietin (loss → anemia), and 1-α-hydroxylase (loss → low active vitamin D → CKD-MBD).

cards.prompt: State the AKI diagnostic criteria.
cards.answer: Creatinine rise ≥50% within 7 days, an absolute rise ≥26.5 µmol/L within 48 h, or oliguria <0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 h.

cards.prompt: What urine findings indicate pre-renal AKI?
cards.answer: Bland sediment, low urine sodium (<20), and FENa <1% — intact tubules avidly reabsorbing sodium in response to hypoperfusion.

cards.prompt: How is post-renal AKI diagnosed and treated?
cards.answer: Renal/bladder ultrasound for hydronephrosis (first-line); relieve the obstruction (catheter, stent, or nephrostomy).

cards.prompt: What causes ATN and what does urine microscopy show?
cards.answer: Prolonged ischemia or nephrotoxins (aminoglycosides, IV contrast, myoglobin); muddy-brown hemegranular casts with FENa >1%.

cards.prompt: Describe acute interstitial nephritis.
cards.answer: Allergic AKI ~7–10 days after a drug (penicillins, fluoroquinolones, PPIs, NSAIDs), ± rash/fever/eosinophilia, with WBCs and WBC casts; treat by stopping the drug (± steroids).

cards.prompt: Match urinary casts to the lesion.
cards.answer: Muddy-brown granular → ATN; WBC casts → AIN; RBC casts → glomerulonephritis (nephritic); bland urine with FENa <1% → pre-renal.

cards.prompt: Define nephrotic syndrome.
cards.answer: Proteinuria >3.5 g/day, hypoalbuminemia, edema, and hyperlipidemia (from podocyte/filtration-barrier injury); also a hypercoagulable state.

cards.prompt: What is the commonest pediatric nephrotic cause and its management?
cards.answer: Minimal change disease — normal light microscopy, foot-process effacement on EM; start empiric oral prednisone (biopsy only if steroid-unresponsive).

cards.prompt: Define nephritic syndrome.
cards.answer: Hematuria with RBC casts, variable proteinuria, hypertension, and reduced GFR from glomerular inflammation/proliferation.

cards.prompt: How does complement help classify nephritic disease?
cards.answer: Low C3/C4 → immune-complex/complement-consuming (SLE, post-infectious GN); normal complement with pulmonary-renal disease → pauci-immune ANCA vasculitis or anti-GBM.

cards.prompt: Why is RPGN an emergency and how is it treated?
cards.answer: It can destroy kidneys within days (crescentic GN); give urgent empiric IV pulse corticosteroids without waiting for the biopsy (± cyclophosphamide/rituximab, plasma exchange).

cards.prompt: What is the CKD-MBD lab pattern and mechanism?
cards.answer: Low calcium, high phosphate, high PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism) — from loss of 1-α-hydroxylase (low active vitamin D) and impaired phosphate excretion.

cards.prompt: List the AEIOU indications for urgent dialysis.
cards.answer: Acidosis (pH <7.1), Electrolytes (refractory hyperkalemia), Intoxication (methanol, lithium, ASA), Overload (refractory pulmonary edema), Uremia (pericarditis, encephalopathy).

cards.prompt: A normal GFR is approximately:
A FENa <1% with bland urine indicates:
Muddy-brown granular casts are characteristic of:
Which drug class classically causes acute interstitial nephritis?
Minimal change disease is the commonest nephrotic cause in:
ANCA-associated vasculitis classically shows:
The most common cause of CKD is:
The target hemoglobin for ESA therapy in CKD is:
Which antihypertensive is contraindicated in pregnancy?
Which agent slows progression of proteinuric diabetic CKD?
Select recognised causes of acute tubular necrosis:
Select features of nephrotic syndrome:
Select nephritic (RBC-cast) glomerular diseases:
Select complications of CKD/uremia:
options: CKD-MBD (low Ca, high PO4, high PTH)

cards.prompt: Select antihypertensives safe in pregnancy:

cards.text: AKI: creatinine rise ≥ {{50}}% in 7 days, or ≥ {{26.5}} µmol/L in 48 h, or oliguria <0.5 mL/kg/h for {{6}} h.
FENa < {{1}}% suggests pre-renal AKI; FENa > 1% suggests {{ATN}}.
cards.explain: Reflects whether tubules can still reabsorb sodium.
Secondary hyperparathyroidism from low active vitamin D + phosphate retention.

cards.text: CKD = eGFR < {{60}} or kidney damage for ≥ {{3}} months.
After starting an ACEi/ARB, a creatinine rise up to {{30}}% is acceptable; watch for {{hyperkalemia}}.

cards.prompt: Order the workup of AKI:
items: Compute FENa; ultrasound to exclude obstruction
Classify pre-renal / intrinsic / post-renal and treat the cause

cards.prompt: Order the response to suspected RPGN:
items: Recognize nephritic syndrome with RBC casts + rising creatinine
Start empiric IV pulse corticosteroids urgently
Obtain serologies (ANCA, anti-GBM, complement) and biopsy
Add cyclophosphamide/rituximab ± plasma exchange per cause

cards.prompt: Order CKD progression management:
items: Add SGLT2 inhibitor for proteinuric CKD

cards.prompt: Match the AKI type to its clue:
Match the glomerular disease to its marker:
Match the drug to its role:
Match the renal hormone to its effect:
What characterizes anti-GBM (Goodpasture) disease?
cards.answer: Antibodies to the GBM causing crescentic glomerulonephritis plus pulmonary hemorrhage (hemoptysis); treat with steroids, cyclophosphamide, and plasma exchange.

cards.prompt: Why is diabetic nephropathy so important?
cards.answer: It is the single most common cause of CKD; chronic hyperglycemia thickens the GBM, expands the mesangium, and causes glomerulosclerosis with progressive albuminuria.

cards.prompt: What clues point to ADPKD?
cards.answer: Strong family history of kidney failure/dialysis, progressive GFR loss with minimal proteinuria, hypertension, large cystic kidneys, hepatic cysts, and intracranial aneurysms.

cards.prompt: What is the main complication of renal biopsy and who is at higher risk?
cards.answer: Bleeding; risk is increased in the frail/elderly, severe hypertension, and small echogenic (chronically scarred) kidneys.

cards.prompt: Outline acute hyperkalemia management.
cards.answer: Stabilize the myocardium with IV calcium (gluconate), shift potassium intracellularly (insulin + glucose, salbutamol, bicarbonate if acidotic), and remove it (potassium binder, diuresis, or dialysis if refractory).

cards.prompt: What are the metabolic adverse effects of thiazide-like diuretics?
cards.answer: Hyponatremia, hypokalemia, and hyperuricemia (and hyperglycemia/hypercalcemia); they cause diuresis followed by vasodilation.

cards.prompt: Which drugs precipitate pre-renal AKI by altering glomerular hemodynamics?
cards.answer: NSAIDs (constrict the afferent arteriole) and ACE inhibitors/ARBs (dilate the efferent arteriole) — both reduce filtration pressure, especially in volume depletion or renal-artery stenosis.

guides.title: Week 33 · 1 Renal Physiology & Evaluation
guides.html:
Foundations

Renal Physiology & Evaluation

Week 33 · KU I — Renal Function, AKI & CKD
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

What the kidney does, how GFR is estimated (and when creatinine lies), and the endocrine roles that explain CKD's complications.

What the kidneys do

Regulate extracellular fluid volume, electrolytes, and acid-base; control blood pressure; support hemoglobin; activate vitamin D; and excrete metabolic waste.

GFR — the master index

  • Normal GFR >90 mL/min; it is the best overall index of kidney health.
  • Inulin clearance is the gold standard; clinically we use serum creatinine (endogenous, freely filtered, but also secreted by the proximal tubule and varying with age/sex/muscle mass).
  • Estimating equations (Cockcroft-Gault, MDRD, CKD-EPI) require creatinine to be in a steady state.
Anuria → eGFR is 0
In acute, evolving, anuric AKI the kidney is filtering nothing, so the true eGFR is 0 mL/min regardless of what an equation calculates — the formulas are invalid because creatinine isn't at steady state. Don't be reassured by a "normal" calculated eGFR in an anuric patient.

Endocrine functions (explain CKD complications)

SecretionRoleLost in CKD →
ReninBP / sodium via RAASHypertension
ErythropoietinRBC production (hypoxia sensor)Anemia
1-α-hydroxylaseActivates vitamin D (1,25-OH)Hypocalcemia, CKD-MBD
GFR is the master index; eGFR equations need a steady-state creatinine (useless in anuric AKI → eGFR 0). The kidney's renin/EPO/vitamin-D roles predict CKD's hypertension, anemia, and bone disease.

guides.title: Week 33 · 2 Acute Kidney Injury: Pre-renal & Post-renal
guides.html:
AKI I

Acute Kidney Injury: Pre-renal & Post-renal

Week 33 · KU I — Renal Function, AKI & CKD
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The AKI definition, the perfusion problem (and its urine signature), and the obstruction you confirm with ultrasound.

Defining AKI

Any of: serum creatinine rise ≥50% in 7 days, an absolute rise ≥26.5 µmol/L in 48 h, or oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/h for 6 h).

Pre-renal AKI (a perfusion problem)

Decreased renal perfusion: absolute hypovolemia, decreased effective circulating volume (heart failure, cirrhosis), or altered hemodynamics (NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors/ARBs). The nephrons are intact and avidly reabsorb sodium and water.

Urine findingPre-renal
SedimentNormal (bland)
Urine sodiumLow (<20 mmol/L)
FENa<1% (avid sodium reabsorption)
FENa logic
A low FENa (<1%) means the tubules are working hard to retain sodium → the kidney itself is fine and is responding to poor perfusion (pre-renal). A high FENa (>1%) means the tubules can't reabsorb sodium → intrinsic damage (ATN).

Post-renal AKI (obstruction)

  • Physical obstruction to urine flow: BPH, nephrolithiasis, pelvic/abdominal malignancy.
  • First-line investigation: renal/bladder ultrasound to look for hydronephrosis; relieving the obstruction (catheter/stent/nephrostomy) can reverse it.
AKI = creatinine up ≥50%/7d or ≥26.5 in 48h, or oliguria. Pre-renal: bland urine, FENa <1% (perfusion). Post-renal: ultrasound for hydronephrosis, then relieve the obstruction.

guides.title: Week 33 · 3 Intrinsic AKI: ATN & AIN
guides.html:
AKI II

Intrinsic AKI: ATN & AIN

Week 33 · KU I — Renal Function, AKI & CKD
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The two intrinsic AKIs that get tested — ischemic/toxic tubular necrosis and allergic interstitial nephritis — by their casts and triggers.

Acute tubular necrosis (ATN)

Direct tubular injury from prolonged ischemia (a pre-renal state that wasn't corrected) or nephrotoxins: aminoglycosides, IV contrast, myoglobin (rhabdomyolysis), cisplatin. Damaged tubules can't concentrate urine or reabsorb sodium.

FindingATN
FENa>1%
Urine microscopy"Muddy brown" hemegranular casts
CourseOliguric → recovery (polyuric) phase

Acute interstitial nephritis (AIN)

An allergic, drug-induced AKI
Typically an allergic reaction to drugs — penicillins, fluoroquinolones, PPIs, NSAIDs (and other antibiotics). Onset ~7–10 days post-exposure, sometimes with rash, fever, eosinophilia. Urinalysis shows WBCs and WBC casts (and sometimes eosinophiluria). The most effective treatment is stopping the offending drug; corticosteroids may be added.

Telling them apart

CluePoints to
Muddy brown granular casts, FENa >1%ATN
WBC casts, eosinophilia, recent new drugAIN
RBC castsGlomerulonephritis (nephritic)
Bland urine, FENa <1%Pre-renal
ATN: ischemia/toxins → muddy brown casts, FENa >1%. AIN: a new drug ~1 week ago → WBC casts ± eosinophilia → stop the drug. The cast tells you the compartment.

guides.title: Week 33 · 4 Glomerular Disease: Nephrotic Syndrome
guides.html:
Nephrotic

Glomerular Disease: Nephrotic Syndrome

Week 33 · KU I — Renal Function, AKI & CKD
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Heavy proteinuria from podocyte injury — the defining tetrad and the causes from children to adults.

The nephrotic picture

Podocyte (filtration-barrier) injury → heavy proteinuria >3.5 g/day, hypoalbuminemia, generalized edema, and hyperlipidemia (high LDL). Also a hypercoagulable state (loss of antithrombin) → VTE risk.

CauseKey feature
Diabetic nephropathyMost common overall; GBM thickening, mesangial expansion, glomerulosclerosis; the leading cause of CKD
Membranous nephropathyAdults; autoantibodies to PLA2R on podocytes → subepithelial immune complexes ("spike and dome")
Minimal change diseaseMost common in children; normal light microscopy, podocyte foot-process effacement on EM; steroid-responsive
FSGSCommon cause in adults (esp. certain populations); segmental scarring
Pediatric nephrotic = treat empirically
In a child with classic nephrotic syndrome (heavy proteinuria, edema, no hematuria, normal BP), the cause is almost always minimal change disease. Start empiric oral prednisone (1 mg/kg) as an outpatient — a biopsy is reserved for steroid non-responders.
Nephrotic = proteinuria >3.5 g + hypoalbuminemia + edema + hyperlipidemia. Kids → minimal change disease → empiric steroids (no biopsy first). Adults → membranous (PLA2R) / FSGS / diabetic.

guides.title: Week 33 · 5 Glomerular Disease: Nephritic Syndrome
guides.html:
Nephritic

Glomerular Disease: Nephritic Syndrome

Week 33 · KU I — Renal Function, AKI & CKD
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Inflammatory glomerular injury — hematuria with RBC casts — and how complement and antibodies split the causes.

The nephritic picture

Glomerular inflammation/proliferation → hematuria with RBC casts, variable proteinuria, hypertension, and a falling GFR.

CauseClue
IgA nephropathyMost common GN worldwide; hematuria (often synpharyngitic), can have normal BP/function early
SLE nephritisAnti-dsDNA, low C3/C4 (complement-consuming), positive ANA
ANCA-associated vasculitisPauci-immune: PR3/MPO-ANCA, normal C3/C4, negative ANA; necrotizing GN
Anti-GBM (Goodpasture)Anti-GBM antibodies; crescentic GN + pulmonary hemorrhage (hemoptysis)
Complement splits the differential
Low C3/C4 → immune-complex/complement-consuming (SLE, post-infectious GN). Normal complement with a pulmonary-renal syndrome → pauci-immune ANCA vasculitis (negative ANA) or anti-GBM disease.
RPGN is a renal emergency
Rapidly progressive GN (nephritic + RBC casts + creatinine rising over days, crescents on biopsy) can destroy kidneys within days. Give urgent IV ("pulse") corticosteroids empirically — do not wait for the biopsy. (Often plus cyclophosphamide or rituximab and plasma exchange for anti-GBM/severe ANCA.)
Nephritic = hematuria + RBC casts + HTN. IgA is commonest; low complement = SLE/post-infectious; normal complement + hemoptysis = ANCA (pauci-immune) or anti-GBM. RPGN → pulse steroids now, biopsy doesn't delay treatment.

guides.title: Week 33 · 6 Chronic Kidney Disease & Complications
guides.html:
CKD

Chronic Kidney Disease & Complications

Week 33 · KU I — Renal Function, AKI & CKD
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The CKD definition, the renoprotection package, and the predictable uremic complications — anemia, bone disease, and electrolytes.

Definition

eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73m² or markers of kidney damage (e.g. albuminuria) present for ≥3 months. Diabetes is the most common cause of CKD.

Delaying progression (cardiovascular-risk reduction)

  • Strict blood pressure control (e.g. systolic <120 for high-risk per SPRINT; <130/80 in diabetes).
  • ACE inhibitor or ARB — slows progression independent of BP (especially with albuminuria).
  • Optimize diabetes; add an SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin) for proteinuric CKD; consider a non-steroidal MRA (finerenone).

Complications of uremia/CKD

ComplicationMechanism
AnemiaLoss of peritubular EPO production → treat with an ESA when Hb <100, target 110–120 (not normal — stroke risk)
CKD-MBD↓1-α-hydroxylase → ↓active vitamin D → low calcium, high phosphate, high PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism)
Electrolyte/acid-baseHyperkalemia and metabolic acidosis (reduced tubular secretion)
Dialysis indications (AEIOU)
Acidosis (pH <7.1), Electrolytes (refractory hyperkalemia), Intoxication (methanol, lithium, ASA, ethylene glycol), Overload (refractory pulmonary edema), Uremia (pericarditis, encephalopathy). In CKD, dialysis is typically started as eGFR falls below ~8–9 or for resistant hyperkalemia.
CKD = eGFR <60 or damage ≥3 months (diabetes #1). Renoprotect with BP control + ACEi/ARB + SGLT2 inhibitor. CKD-MBD = low Ca, high PO4, high PTH. Dialyze for AEIOU.

guides.title: Week 33 · 7 Hypertension & Renoprotective Pharmacology
guides.html:
BP drugs

Hypertension & Renoprotective Pharmacology

Week 33 · KU I — Renal Function, AKI & CKD
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The first-line antihypertensive classes, the ACEi/ARB rules, and what's safe in pregnancy.

First-line classes

Long-acting thiazide-like diuretics, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and long-acting calcium-channel blockers. Lifestyle: the DASH diet gives the greatest BP-lowering of any single lifestyle change.

ClassNotes / adverse effects
ACE inhibitors ("-prils") / ARBs ("-sartans")Block RAAS, renoprotective. Watch hyperkalemia and creatinine (a <30% rise is acceptable). ACEi cough/angioedema (bradykinin). Both contraindicated in pregnancy.
Thiazide-like (indapamide, chlorthalidone)Diuresis then vasodilation; hyponatremia, hypokalemia, hyperuricemia
Dihydropyridine CCB (amlodipine)Vasodilation; dose-dependent peripheral edema
Non-dihydropyridine CCB (verapamil, diltiazem)↓Heart rate; constipation. (Both classes can cause gingival hyperplasia.)
Pregnancy
Safe: labetalol, methyldopa, nifedipine XL. Contraindicated: ACE inhibitors and ARBs (teratogenic — renal dysgenesis). This is a recurring exam point.

Renoprotection beyond BP

  • ACEi/ARB (especially with albuminuria), SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin) for proteinuric CKD, and a non-steroidal MRA (finerenone) in diabetic CKD slow progression beyond their BP effect.
First-line: thiazide-like, ACEi/ARB, CCB. ACEi/ARB are renoprotective but raise K+ and are contraindicated in pregnancy (use labetalol/methyldopa/nifedipine). DASH diet is the top lifestyle lever.

guides.title: Week 33 · 8 Special Populations, Cystic Disease & Dialysis
guides.html:
Edge cases

Special Populations, Cystic Disease & Dialysis

Week 33 · KU I — Renal Function, AKI & CKD
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

ADPKD, the geriatric kidney and dosing, EPO targets, and renal-biopsy risk.

Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD)

  • Genetic tubulointerstitial CKD: abnormal polycystin → massive cysts compress normal tissue.
  • Clues: strong family history of dialysis/kidney failure, progressive GFR loss, minimal proteinuria, hypertension, and extrarenal features (hepatic cysts, intracranial aneurysms).
  • Tolvaptan (a vaptan) can slow cyst growth in selected rapidly-progressing patients.

The geriatric kidney

Dose by GFR, not by creatinine alone
Aging reduces functional glomeruli by 30–50%, but low muscle mass keeps creatinine deceptively "normal." Always estimate GFR before renally-cleared drugs (e.g. antibiotics) in older adults. Pharmacokinetics shift: unpredictable absorption, larger volume of distribution for free drugs (low albumin), reduced hepatic/renal clearance.

EPO therapy targets

  • ESA for symptomatic anemia with Hb <100, target 110–120 g/L. Do not normalize Hb (>130) — linked to increased stroke. Ensure iron stores are adequate first.

Dialysis in the elderly & biopsy risk

  • In patients >80 with ESRD, hemodialysis offers only modest longevity gains over conservative management — shared decision-making matters.
  • Renal biopsy: bleeding is the commonest complication; risk is much higher in the frail/elderly, severe hypertension, and small echogenic (chronic) kidneys.
ADPKD: family history + big cysts + minimal proteinuria (± brain aneurysm). Estimate GFR before dosing in elders. EPO target 110–120 (never normalize). Biopsy's main risk is bleeding.

guides.title: Week 33 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

KU I — Renal Function, AKI & CKD

Week 33 · KU I — Renal Function, AKI & CKD
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Refractory hyperkalemia / acidosis / overload / uremia → urgent dialysis (AEIOU)
RPGN (nephritic + RBC casts + rising creatinine) → urgent empiric IV pulse steroids — don't wait for biopsy
Pulmonary-renal syndrome + hemoptysis → ANCA vasculitis or anti-GBM (check complement/ANCA/anti-GBM)
Acute hyperkalemia with ECG changes → IV calcium first, then shift (insulin/glucose) + remove
ACEi/ARB in pregnancy → contraindicated (teratogen) — use labetalol/methyldopa/nifedipine
Anuric AKI → true eGFR is 0 regardless of the calculated value

Physiology Physiology

GFR best index; normal >90
Creatinine needs steady state; secreted by PCT
Anuria eGFR = 0 (equations invalid)
Renin/EPO/vit D → HTN / anemia / CKD-MBD

AKI AKI

Definition Cr ↑≥50%/7d, ≥26.5/48h, or oliguria
Pre-renal bland urine, FENa <1%
ATN muddy brown casts, FENa >1% (toxins/ischemia)
AIN WBC casts, drug ~7d, eosinophilia → stop drug
Post-renal ultrasound → hydronephrosis → relieve

Nephrotic Nephrotic

Definition proteinuria >3.5, low albumin, edema, ↑lipids
Children minimal change → empiric steroids
Adults membranous (PLA2R), FSGS, diabetic
Diabetic nephropathy #1 cause of CKD

Nephritic Nephritic

Definition hematuria + RBC casts + HTN + ↓GFR
IgA commonest GN; synpharyngitic
Low complement SLE, post-infectious
Normal complement ANCA (pauci-immune), anti-GBM
RPGN pulse steroids now, don't wait for biopsy

CKD CKD

Definition eGFR <60 or damage ≥3 months
Renoprotect BP + ACEi/ARB + SGLT2 (+ finerenone)
CKD-MBD low Ca, high PO4, high PTH
Anemia EPO when Hb <100, target 110–120
Dialysis AEIOU; CKD start ~eGFR 8–9

HTN & pregnancy BP

First-line thiazide-like, ACEi/ARB, CCB
ACEi/ARB ↑K, <30% Cr rise OK, cough/angioedema
Pregnancy safe labetalol, methyldopa, nifedipine XL
Pregnancy avoid ACEi/ARB (teratogen)
Lifestyle DASH diet best; diabetes target <130/80

Special Edge cases

ADPKD family hx, big cysts, minimal proteinuria, berry aneurysm
Geriatrics estimate GFR before dosing
EPO target 110–120, never normalize
Biopsy bleeding is the main risk

facets.adverse: Hyperkalemia, cough, angioedema; <30% creatinine rise acceptable
